title: Charmed Life - Chapter 7 (Fragment 1)
text:
Chapter Four

4

T
 HE SAME SOFTNESS and silence were there when the red-haired Mary woke Cat the next morning and told him it was time to get up. Bright morning sunshine was flooding the curved walls of his room. Though Cat knew now that the Castle must be full of people, he could hear not a sound from any of them. Nor could he hear anything from outside the windows.

I know what it’s like! Cat thought. It’s like when it’s snowed in the night. The idea made him feel so pleased and so warm that he went to sleep again.

“You really must get up, Eric,” Mary said, shaking him. “I’ve run your bath, and your lessons start at nine. Make haste, or you won’t have time for breakfast.”

Cat got up. He had so strong a feeling that it had snowed in the night that he was quite surprised to find his room warm in the sun. He looked out of the windows, and there were green lawns and flowers, and rooks circling the green trees, as if there had been some mistake. Mary had gone. Cat was glad, because he was not at all sure he liked her, and he was afraid of missing breakfast. When he was dressed, he went along to the bathroom and let the hot water out of the bath. Then he dashed down the twisting stairs to find Gwendolen.

“Where do we go for breakfast?” he asked her anxiously.

Gwendolen was never at her best in the morning. She was sitting on her blue velvet stool in front of her garlanded mirror, crossly combing her golden hair. Combing her hair was another thing which always made her cross. “I don’t know and I don’t care! Shut up!” she said.

“Now that’s no way to speak,” said the maid called Euphemia, briskly following Cat into the room. She was rather a pretty girl, and she did not seem to find her name the burden it should have been. “We’re waiting to give you breakfast along here. Come on.”

Gwendolen hurled her comb down expressively and they followed Euphemia to a room just along the corridor. It was a square, airy room, with a row of big windows but, compared with the rest of the Castle, it was rather shabby. The leather chairs were battered. The grassy carpet had stains on it. None of the cupboards would shut properly. Things like clockwork trains and tennis rackets bulged out. Julia and Roger were sitting waiting at a table by the windows, in clothes as shabby as the room.

Mary, who was waiting there too, said, “And about time!” and began to work an interesting lift in a cupboard by the fireplace. There was a clank. Mary opened the lift and fetched out a large plate of bread and butter and a steaming brown jug of cocoa. She brought these over to the table and Euphemia poured each child a mug of the cocoa.

Gwendolen stared from her mug to the plate of bread. “Is this all there is?”

“What else do you want?” asked Euphemia.

Gwendolen could not find words to express what she wanted. Porridge, bacon and eggs, grapefruit, toast and kippers all occurred to her at once, and she went on staring.

“Make up your mind,” Euphemia said at last. “My breakfast’s waiting for me too, you know.”

“Isn’t there any marmalade?” said Gwendolen.

Euphemia and Mary looked at one another. “Julia and Roger are not allowed marmalade,” Mary said.

“Nobody forbade me to have it,” said Gwendolen. “Get me some marmalade at once.”

Mary went to a speaking tube by the lift and, after much rumbling and another clank, a pot of marmalade arrived. Mary brought it and put it in front of Gwendolen.

vocabulary.definition: Spacious, well ventilated, and light.
vocabulary.example: The room was described as square and airy.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner resulting from anxiety; nervously.
vocabulary.example: Cat anxiously asked Gwendolen about breakfast.

vocabulary.definition: Worn out or damaged by use or age.
vocabulary.example: The leather chairs were battered.

vocabulary.definition: In an active, quick, or energetic way.
vocabulary.example: Euphemia followed Cat briskly.

vocabulary.definition: To swell or stick out over a surface.
vocabulary.example: Items bulged out of cupboards.

vocabulary.definition: Moving around in a circle or circuit.
vocabulary.example: There were rooks circling the green trees.

vocabulary.definition: A loud, sharp sound, typically made by metal hitting metal.
vocabulary.example: The clank was heard as the cupboard opened.

vocabulary.definition: A long passage in a building from which doors lead into rooms.
vocabulary.example: The corridor led them to the dining room.

vocabulary.definition: In an annoyed or angry manner.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen was crossly combing her golden hair.

vocabulary.definition: Having a smoothly rounded shape rather than straight lines.
vocabulary.example: Bright morning sunshine was flooding the curved walls of his room.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner that clearly conveys a feeling or meaning.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen hurled her comb down expressively.

vocabulary.definition: To fill or cover a place in large amounts, typically with light or water.
vocabulary.example: Bright morning sunshine was flooding the curved walls of his room.

vocabulary.definition: Decorated with a garland (a wreath or chain of flowers).
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen was sitting in front of her garlanded mirror.

vocabulary.definition: A large, round citrus fruit with a thick yellow skin and slightly sour pulp.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen thought of grapefruit for breakfast.

vocabulary.definition: Excessive speed or urgency of movement or action; hurry.
vocabulary.example: Make haste, or you won’t have time for breakfast.

vocabulary.definition: Fish, usually herring, that has been split, salted, and smoked.
vocabulary.example: Kippers occurred to Gwendolen as breakfast choices.

vocabulary.definition: A compartment that moves people or goods up and down in a building; an elevator.
vocabulary.example: Mary operated a lift in the cupboard by the fireplace.

vocabulary.definition: A female domestic worker.
vocabulary.example: A maid named Euphemia helped the children.

vocabulary.definition: A preserve made from citrus fruit, especially oranges, including the peel.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen asked for marmalade.

vocabulary.definition: A large cup with a handle, used for hot drinks.
vocabulary.example: Children drank cocoa from mugs.

vocabulary.definition: A soft food made by boiling oats in milk or water.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen imagined porridge at breakfast.

vocabulary.definition: In poor condition through long use or lack of care.
vocabulary.example: The room was shabby compared with the rest of the Castle.

vocabulary.definition: A tube used in old buildings to communicate between rooms by speaking into it.
vocabulary.example: Mary used a speaking tube to request marmalade.

vocabulary.definition: Emitting steam; very hot.
vocabulary.example: Mary fetched a steaming brown jug of cocoa.

vocabulary.definition: Having many turns or bends, not straight.
vocabulary.example: He dashed down the twisting stairs to find Gwendolen.
